start.title: The Afternoon Shift
start.text:
Theodore Ernest O'Hare — Theo to everyone who knows him — is seventeen, restless, and supposed to be writing an essay on American **neutrality**. His eyelids are heavy. The library smells of old paper and floor polish.

When Theo opens his eyes, the fluorescent lights are gone. Gas lamps hiss overhead. The books on the shelves are bound in cloth and leather. A newspaper on the reading table says April 2, 1917. The headline screams: PRESIDENT TO ADDRESS CONGRESS TONIGHT.

A man in a rumpled suit and round spectacles looks up from the next table. He has the build of a beer barrel and the expression of someone who has just bitten into a lemon. 'You look lost, boy,' he says. 'Sit down before you fall down.'

This, though Theo does not yet know it, is H.L. Mencken — journalist, critic, and the most dangerous writer in America.

choices.text: Sit down and ask the man who he is
Grab the newspaper and read the headline

vocabulary.definition: The policy of not taking sides in a conflict; the U.S. was officially neutral from 1914 to 1917

act1_meet_mencken.title: The Sage of Baltimore
act1_meet_mencken.text:
'I'm Henry Mencken,' says the man, not bothering to stand. 'I write for the Baltimore Sun and the Smart Set, and I have very little patience for fools. Which are you — a fool, or something more interesting?'

Before Theo can answer, Mencken waves a thick hand. 'Never mind. Everyone's a fool tonight. Wilson's about to drag this country into a European slaughterhouse, and half the nation will cheer because he'll wrap it in pretty words.'

He leans forward. 'Do you know what **propaganda** is, boy? Not the crude kind — not posters of gorillas in German helmets. I mean the real thing. The kind that sounds like philosophy. The kind that makes men march to their deaths believing they're marching toward God.'

Mencken taps the newspaper. 'Wilson speaks tonight. Would you like to learn how to listen — really listen — to what a politician says? Or would you rather believe every word, like the rest of the **booboisie**?'

choices.text: 'Teach me how to listen.'
'What's wrong with believing in something?'
'I want to hear Wilson's speech first.'

vocabulary.definition: Information, often biased or misleading, used to promote a cause or point of view
Mencken's coined term for the gullible American middle class (a blend of 'boob' and 'bourgeoisie')

act1_read_paper.title: The Headlines Roar
act1_read_paper.text:
Theo picks up the newspaper. The front page is dense with war news:

PRESIDENT TO ADDRESS CONGRESS — WAR EXPECTED
GERMAN U-BOATS SINK THREE AMERICAN SHIPS
ZIMMERMANN TELEGRAM REVEALED: MEXICO OFFERED U.S. TERRITORY

The **editorial** columns are even more striking. One column argues that America must fight to defend civilization itself. Another warns that war will only enrich munitions makers. A third quotes a Harvard professor who says combat builds character in young men.

'Three opinions,' says the man at the next table, peering over his spectacles. 'Three completely different reasons to support — or oppose — the same war. Interesting, isn't it? Each one sounds perfectly reasonable. Each one uses different **rhetorical** tricks to get you nodding along.'

He extends a meaty hand. 'Henry Mencken. And you have the look of someone who might actually think before he enlists.'

choices.text: 'How can three arguments all sound right?'
Ask about the professor who says war builds character
Ask about the editorial on war profiteering

vocabulary.definition: A newspaper article expressing the opinion of the editor or publisher, rather than reporting facts
Relating to rhetoric — the art of using language effectively and persuasively

act1_lesson_rhetoric.title: Mencken's First Lesson
act1_lesson_rhetoric.text:
Mencken pulls a battered notebook from his coat. 'Lesson one. Every argument has three legs — the Greeks called them **ethos**, **pathos**, and **logos**. The speaker's character, the audience's emotions, and the actual reasoning.'

He scrawls on a napkin:

ETHOS: 'Trust me — I'm a war hero / president / professor.'
PATHOS: 'Think of the women and children! Think of honor!'
LOGOS: 'Here are the facts. Here is the evidence.'

'Most propaganda,' Mencken says, 'leans hard on ethos and pathos and pretends that logos is along for the ride. The trick is to spot which leg is doing the real work.'

He fixes Theo with a sharp look. 'Now. There are four voices you need to hear tonight — four men who will try to convince you about war. Each uses these tools differently. Who do you want to hear first?'

choices.text: The president — Woodrow Wilson
The soldier-philosopher who glorifies war
The general who says war is a racket

vocabulary.definition: Appeal to the speaker's credibility, character, or authority — 'Trust me because of who I am'
Appeal to the audience's emotions — fear, anger, pity, pride — to persuade
Appeal to logic and evidence — facts, statistics, and reasoned argument

act1_challenge_mencken.title: The Skeptic Challenged
act1_challenge_mencken.text:
'What's wrong with believing?' Theo asks. 'People are dying over there. Maybe we should do something about it.'

Mencken's eyebrows rise. 'Ah. A young idealist. Fine — I don't object to idealism. I object to stupidity dressed up as idealism.' He counts on his fingers:

'One: I don't say war is never necessary. I say the reasons politicians give for war are almost always lies dressed in Sunday clothes.

'Two: I don't say you shouldn't care. I say you should care enough to think clearly, even when everyone around you is howling for blood.

'Three: The most dangerous **demagoguery** is the kind that sounds noble. A good **demagogue** doesn't shout — he speaks in measured tones about sacrifice and duty and the arc of civilization.'

He pauses. 'So. Will you listen with your brain turned on? Or do you want to go wave a flag?'

choices.text: 'Teach me to think clearly about this.'
'I want to hear the case for fighting.'
'I want to hear the case against war.'

vocabulary.definition: The practice of gaining political power by appealing to people's emotions, prejudices, and desires rather than their reason
A political leader who seeks support by appealing to emotions and prejudices rather than rational argument

act2_wilson_speech.title: The War Address
act2_wilson_speech.text:
The Capitol rotunda is packed. President Wilson stands at the podium, pale and composed. His voice carries through the chamber:

'The world must be made safe for democracy. Its peace must be planted upon the tested foundations of political liberty.'

The crowd erupts. Senators pound their desks. Even the skeptics are on their feet.

Mencken, standing beside Theo in the gallery, whispers: 'Listen to the **diction** — safe, foundations, liberty. Every word is chosen to make war sound like architecture. He's not asking them to kill — he's asking them to build.'

Wilson continues: 'We have no selfish ends to serve. We desire no conquest, no dominion.'

'There,' Mencken murmurs. 'The **antithesis**. No conquest, no dominion. He tells you what we're not doing, so you forget to ask what we are doing.'

choices.text: Ask Mencken: 'But what if Wilson is sincere?'
Ask Mencken to explain more rhetorical techniques
Leave the Capitol — you want to hear a different voice

vocabulary.definition: The choice of words a speaker or writer uses; word choice that shapes tone, meaning, and persuasive power
A rhetorical device that places contrasting ideas side by side, often in parallel structure, for emphasis

act2_wilson_sincere.title: Sincerity and Strategy
act2_wilson_sincere.text:
'Sincere?' Mencken chews the word like it tastes bad. 'Probably. That's what makes him dangerous. A cynical liar you can catch. A man who sincerely believes his own propaganda? That's the one who'll send a million boys to France without blinking.'

He pulls out the napkin from earlier. 'Look — Wilson's speech is almost pure **pathos** and **ethos**. His ethos: I'm the President, I've kept us out of war for three years, trust my judgment. His pathos: democracy, liberty, the rights of mankind.'

'But where's the **logos**? Where are the numbers? How many will die? What will it cost? What happens if we win? He doesn't say. He can't say. Because the moment you do the math, the poetry falls apart.'

Theo looks back at Wilson, still speaking. The crowd is weeping.

choices.text: 'Maybe some things are worth fighting for without doing the math.'
'Who else has something to say about this war?'
'Show me the math — who profits from war?'

vocabulary.definition: Appeal to the audience's emotions — fear, anger, pity, pride — to persuade
Appeal to the speaker's credibility, character, or authority
Appeal to logic and evidence — facts, statistics, and reasoned argument

act2_wilson_rhetoric.title: The Machinery of the Speech
act2_wilson_rhetoric.text:
Mencken is in his element now. He ticks off techniques on his fingers as Wilson speaks:

'**Anaphora** — he repeats the same structure at the start of each sentence. We shall fight... We shall fight... It creates rhythm, like a drumbeat. Your heart starts marching before your brain catches up.'

'**Euphemism** — he never says killing. He says making the world safe. He never says invasion. He says vindication of human right. The bloodier the reality, the prettier the language.'

'**Appeal to higher authority** — not God exactly, but civilization itself. He makes America a character in a moral drama. You're not voting for war — you're voting for the future of the human race.'

Mencken looks tired. 'It's brilliant, I'll give him that. Every **register** shift is calculated — high philosophy when he wants you to feel noble, plain speech when he wants you to feel like a neighbor being asked for help.'

choices.text: 'Could these techniques be used for good, not just manipulation?'
'I want to hear someone who disagrees with Wilson.'
Accept Wilson's case — the world needs saving

vocabulary.definition: Repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses or sentences, used for emphasis and rhythm
A mild or indirect word substituted for one considered too harsh or blunt — e.g., 'passed away' for 'died'
A rhetorical strategy that invokes a power above the speaker — God, civilization, history — to make the argument seem beyond question
The level of formality in language — high register (formal, elevated) vs. low register (casual, plain)

act2_holmes_intro.title: The Soldier's Creed
act2_holmes_intro.text:
Mencken hands Theo a worn pamphlet. 'Read this. Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. — Supreme Court Justice, wounded three times in the Civil War. This is his Memorial Day speech from 1895.'

Theo reads: '...the faith is true and adorable which leads a soldier to throw away his life in obedience to a blindly accepted duty, in a cause which he little understands...'

'**Martial virtue**,' Mencken says. 'Holmes doesn't just argue that war is sometimes necessary. He argues that war is good — that it builds something in men that peace and commerce can't. He calls it the soldier's faith.'

Theo looks at the words again: blindly accepted duty... a cause which he little understands.

'Notice,' Mencken says, 'that Holmes is openly celebrating **blind obedience**. He's not hiding it. He thinks not understanding the cause is the point. The sacrifice is more pure when the soldier doesn't ask why.'

choices.text: 'There's something noble about that, isn't there?'
'That's terrifying. Who would throw away their life blindly?'
'How does Holmes's argument compare to Wilson's?'

vocabulary.definition: The belief that military qualities — courage, discipline, self-sacrifice — are among the highest human virtues
Following orders or accepting duty without questioning the cause — Holmes celebrates this as the essence of the soldier's faith

act2_holmes_noble.title: The Romance of Sacrifice
act2_holmes_noble.text:
Mencken studies Theo carefully. 'You feel it, don't you? The pull of it. Holmes is a magnificent writer — he makes death in battle sound like a sacrament.'

He reads aloud: 'Through our great good fortune, in our youth our hearts were touched with fire. It was given to us to learn at the outset that life is a profound and passionate thing.'

'**Touched with fire**,' Mencken repeats. 'That's not an argument. That's **poetry**. And poetry is harder to resist than logic, because it bypasses the brain entirely.'

He leans back. 'Here's my question for you, Theo. Holmes was genuinely brave — shot through the neck at Antietam, nearly died. Does his personal courage make his argument stronger? Or does his **ethos** as a wounded veteran make you accept claims you'd reject from anyone else?'

choices.text: 'His experience makes his argument credible.'
'You're right — I'm being swayed by who he is, not what he says.'
'I want to hear from someone who saw war differently.'

vocabulary.definition: Holmes's famous phrase for the transformative experience of combat — making war sound sacred rather than brutal
Language that appeals to emotion and imagination rather than reason — Mencken warns it bypasses the brain
Appeal to the speaker's credibility, character, or authority — here, Holmes's war wounds lend weight to his words

act2_holmes_critique.title: Against Blind Obedience
act2_holmes_critique.text:
'Good,' Mencken says. 'You're thinking. Most people hear touched with fire and their brains shut off.'

He pulls out another document. 'Now compare Holmes to this — it's from the trenches of France, 1916. A German soldier named Remarque wrote it later, but the reality was already there:'

He reads: 'We see men living with their skulls blown open; we see soldiers run with their two feet cut off... And this is what Holmes calls being touched with fire.'

'Holmes never describes what war actually looks like,' Mencken says quietly. 'He uses **abstraction** — faith, duty, fire, the profound and passionate. Never blood. Never mud. Never gangrene. That's not an accident. The moment you get **concrete**, the romance dies.'

'The gap between Holmes's poetry and Remarque's reality — that gap is where propaganda lives.'

choices.text: 'Who profits from that gap?'
'Is there anyone who talks about war honestly?'
'Teach me more about abstraction vs. concrete language.'

vocabulary.definition: Language that refers to concepts, qualities, or ideas (honor, duty, freedom) rather than concrete physical reality
Language that describes specific, physical, sensory details — blood, mud, shattered bone — the opposite of abstraction

act2_butler_intro.title: The General Speaks
act2_butler_intro.text:
Mencken leads Theo to a labor hall where a crowd has gathered. On the stage stands a thick-necked man with a chest full of medals — Major General Smedley Butler, the most decorated Marine in American history.

'War is a racket,' Butler says flatly. 'It always has been. It is possibly the oldest, easily the most profitable, surely the most vicious.'

The crowd murmurs. Butler continues: 'A racket is best described as something that is not what it seems to the majority of the people. Only a small inside group knows what it is about. It is conducted for the benefit of the very few, at the expense of the very many.'

Mencken nudges Theo. 'Listen to his **diction**. Racket, vicious, conducted. He's using the language of crime — gangsters and con men. He's reframing war from a noble crusade into a **confidence game**.'

choices.text: 'Is he right? Is it really just about money?'
'But Butler was a war hero himself — why would he say this?'
'This sounds too simple. War can't just be a racket.'

vocabulary.definition: The choice of words a speaker or writer uses — Butler's criminal vocabulary (racket, vicious) reframes war as a con job
A swindle in which the victim is persuaded to trust the swindler; a con — Butler calls war a 'racket' using this framing

act2_butler_profits.title: Following the Money
act2_butler_profits.text:
Butler pulls out numbers. 'In the World War, 21,000 new millionaires and billionaires were made in the United States. How many of those millionaires served in the trenches?'

He names names — steel companies, shipping companies, meatpackers. 'The profits of the DuPont company went from $6 million before the war to $58 million during.'

Mencken whispers to Theo: 'Now watch — Butler's using pure **logos** here. Numbers. Names. Dollars. No poetry, no abstractions. Just the arithmetic of who gets rich and who gets dead.'

'But notice what he's not doing,' Mencken adds. 'He's not addressing Holmes's argument at all. Holmes says war builds character. Butler says war builds fortunes. They're talking past each other — about the same war, but measuring completely different things.'

Butler's voice rises: 'Who pays the bills? The soldiers — and the people at home. We would sell them their coffins if there was any profit in it.'

choices.text: 'Butler has the facts. The others are just selling dreams.'
'But facts alone don't tell the whole story, do they?'
'I need to hear all four voices before I decide.'

vocabulary.definition: Appeal to logic and evidence — Butler uses hard numbers and dollars to make his case

act2_butler_credibility.title: The Paradox of the War Hero
act2_butler_credibility.text:
'That's exactly the point,' Mencken says, grinning. 'Butler has the most powerful **ethos** in this debate. Two Medals of Honor. Thirty-three years of service. If a pacifist professor said war is a racket, you'd dismiss him. But when the most decorated Marine in America says it? That's ethos doing heavy lifting.'

He pauses. 'Holmes has ethos too — wounded three times, Supreme Court Justice. And Wilson has the ethos of the presidency itself. So you have three men with unimpeachable credentials telling you three completely different things.'

'Which means,' Mencken says slowly, 'that ethos is not enough. A man's **credibility** can make you listen. But it can't tell you who's right. For that, you need to look at the argument itself — the **logos** underneath the uniform.'

choices.text: 'So how do I evaluate the argument itself?'
'I've heard enough voices. Help me think this through.'
'I still want to hear Wilson's speech.'

vocabulary.definition: Appeal to the speaker's credibility — all three men (Butler, Holmes, Wilson) have powerful ethos, yet disagree completely
The quality of being trusted and believed in; in rhetoric, credibility is a key component of ethos
Appeal to logic and evidence — Mencken says you must look past ethos to the argument underneath

act2_crossroads.title: The Voices Converge
act2_crossroads.text:
Theo sits in Mencken's study, surrounded by newspapers, pamphlets, and the ghosts of a hundred arguments. Four voices echo in his mind:

Holmes: War ennobles. Sacrifice is the highest virtue. The soldier's faith transcends reason.

Wilson: War is duty — not for conquest, but to make the world safe for democracy. America must act.

Butler: War is a racket. Follow the money. The soldiers die; the corporations profit.

Remarque (in fragments Mencken has shared): War is mud and blood and shattered boys. All the speeches are lies told from safe distances.

Mencken watches Theo. 'You've heard them all. Now what?'

choices.text: 'Teach me to see through all of them.'
'Holmes is right — some things are worth dying for.'
'Wilson is right — democracy must be defended.'
'Butler is right — it's all about money.'

act3_tools_ethics.title: The Writer's Arsenal
act3_tools_ethics.text:
Mencken spreads his notes across the desk. 'All right, boy. You want the tools. Here they are — the full arsenal of persuasion. But remember: every tool can build or destroy.'

He writes on a fresh sheet:

THE WRITER'S TOOLKIT:
1. **Diction** — word choice shapes reality (racket vs. crusade)
2. **Syntax** — sentence structure creates rhythm and emphasis
3. **Register** — formal vs. plain controls how the audience feels
4. **Figures of speech** — metaphor, anaphora, antithesis do emotional work that logic can't
5. **Abstraction vs. concrete** — hide or reveal the physical truth

'These are morally neutral,' Mencken says. 'Wilson uses them. Holmes uses them. Butler uses them. I use them. The question is: do you use them to illuminate or to deceive?'

He looks at Theo hard. 'What will you do with these tools?'

choices.text: 'I want to use them to expose propaganda — to help people see clearly.'
'I want to write — powerfully, like Mencken does.'
'I want to understand them so no one can manipulate me.'
'Couldn't I use them to convince people of what's right?'

vocabulary.definition: Word choice — the single most powerful tool in the writer's arsenal; racket vs. crusade changes everything
The arrangement of words and phrases to create well-formed sentences; sentence structure as a rhetorical tool
The level of formality in language — mixing high and low register keeps the reader off-balance
Expressions that use words in non-literal or unusual ways for rhetorical effect — metaphor, simile, irony, etc.
The choice between idea-language (honor, duty) and physical-language (blood, mud) — propagandists prefer abstraction

act3_critical_path.title: The Skeptic's Apprentice
act3_critical_path.text:
Mencken nods approvingly. 'Good. A man who can read propaganda is a man who can't be enslaved by it.'

He hands Theo four documents — Wilson's speech, Holmes's Memorial Day address, Butler's testimony, and a page of Remarque. 'Your test. Read all four. For each one, identify:

'Which appeals dominate — ethos, pathos, or logos?
'What concrete realities does each speaker hide or reveal?
'What would each speaker say to the others?
'And most important — what is each speaker asking you to do, and what will it cost you?'

Theo works through the night. By morning, he sees it — the machinery behind each voice. Not that any of them are simply wrong. But that each tells a partial truth and presents it as the whole truth.

'The honest reader,' Mencken says, 'holds all four truths at once and refuses to let any one of them become the only truth.'

choices.text: 'I understand now. All of them have something real — and something missing.'
'But at some point, you have to choose a side.'

act3_writer_path.title: The Craft of Provocation
act3_writer_path.text:
Mencken leans back. 'You want to write? Then write. But understand what good writing is. It's not decoration. It's not showing off your vocabulary. Good writing is a **stiletto** — thin, sharp, and aimed at something.'

He pulls out his own work. 'Look at my sentences. Short ones hit like jabs. Long ones build up pressure until the reader is gasping. I mix high vocabulary with low slang — that's **register mixing** — because it keeps the reader off-balance. Expecting philosophy, getting a punch in the nose.'

He writes a sentence: 'Democracy is the theory that the common people know what they want, and deserve to get it good and hard.'

'See? Starts formal — theory, common people. Ends with a punch — good and hard. That's the Mencken method.'

'But here's the question, boy: will you use this power to tell the truth? Or to sell something?'

choices.text: 'To tell the truth, even when it's unpopular.'
'To convince people of what I believe is right.'
'I'm not sure yet — I need to think about what I believe.'

vocabulary.definition: A thin-bladed dagger — Mencken's metaphor for good writing: precise, sharp, aimed at a target
Deliberately shifting between formal and informal language to surprise the reader and keep them off-balance

act3_persuader_path.title: The Power of Persuasion
act3_persuader_path.text:
Mencken's expression shifts. 'Convince people of what's right. That's what they all say — Holmes, Wilson, Butler. Every one of them believes he's on the side of truth.'

He stands and paces. 'The difference between a great writer and a **propagandist** is this: the writer shows you the evidence and lets you judge. The propagandist arranges the evidence so there's only one possible conclusion — his.'

'Wilson doesn't lie in his speech. But he selects. He chooses which facts to present and which to hide. That's the most sophisticated propaganda there is.'

He turns to Theo. 'So I ask again: will you show people the full picture and trust them to think? Or will you arrange the picture so they think what you want?'

choices.text: 'Show the full picture. Trust the audience.'
'Sometimes people need to be guided to the right answer.'
'I want to go back and look at all the voices one more time.'

vocabulary.definition: A person who spreads propaganda — information designed to promote a particular point of view rather than present balanced truth

act3_martial_path.title: The Call to Arms
act3_martial_path.text:
Theo feels it — the pull Holmes described. Something in him wants to answer a call, to be part of something larger than himself. The recruiting posters on every wall suddenly look less like propaganda and more like invitations.

Mencken watches him carefully. 'I see it in your face. You're hearing the drums. Let me ask you one question before you march off:

'Holmes says the soldier's faith is beautiful because it's blind. Wilson says the cause is just. Butler says it's a swindle. Remarque says it's a slaughter.

'Which of these truths are you choosing to ignore right now? Because ignoring the others doesn't make them false. It just makes you a man who chose not to look.'

choices.text: 'I'm not ignoring them. I've weighed them and chosen.'
'...Maybe I should look again before I decide.'
'You're right. I'm being seduced by the poetry.'

act3_idealist_path.title: The Crusader's Choice
act3_idealist_path.text:
Theo thinks about Wilson's words — the world must be made safe for democracy. There's something real in that. Autocracy is real. The Kaiser's armies are real. The Lusitania's dead are real.

Mencken sighs. 'I knew you'd go this way. You're young — the young always believe in crusades.' But his voice is gentler now.

'If you're going to be an idealist, at least be an honest one. Don't pretend the cost isn't real. Don't use Wilson's **euphemisms** — don't say making the world safe when you mean sending men to die in trenches. Own the full truth of what you're choosing.'

He pauses. 'And when the war is over, and the world is not safe, and democracy has not been saved — don't pretend it was. That's when the real test comes.'

choices.text: 'I'll fight for democracy with my eyes open.'
'Your cynicism is as one-sided as Wilson's idealism.'
'Maybe you're right — maybe fighting isn't the answer.'

vocabulary.definition: Mild or indirect words substituted for harsh ones — Wilson says 'making the world safe' instead of 'sending men to die'

act3_antiwar_path.title: The Dissenter's Road
act3_antiwar_path.text:
Butler's numbers are ringing in Theo's ears. Twenty-one thousand new millionaires. Boys in trenches making thirty dollars a month while steel companies make millions.

'If you go this route,' Mencken warns, 'you'll be unpopular. In wartime, the dissenter is called a traitor. I know — I've been called one for three years. The mob doesn't distinguish between opposing a war and opposing the troops.'

He taps the table. 'Butler's argument is the strongest on **logos** — the numbers are real. But he has a weakness too. He reduces everything to economics. Some men really do fight for something beyond money. Holmes was one of them. Remarque's soldiers were another. To say it's all a racket is to **oversimplify** — to flatten human nature into one dimension.'

'The question is: can you oppose the war without becoming as one-sided as the people you're opposing?'

choices.text: 'Yes — I can oppose the war and still respect the soldiers.'
'You keep saying everyone is partly wrong. Isn't that just paralysis?'
'Maybe no single voice has the full answer.'

vocabulary.definition: Appeal to logic and evidence — Butler's numbers are real, but logos alone can't capture the full human truth of war
To describe or explain something in such simple terms that important complexities are lost or distorted

act3_synthesis_begin.title: The Hardest Lesson
act3_synthesis_begin.text:
Mencken pours two cups of coffee — black, bitter. It's late, and the city outside hums with a nation on the edge of war.

'You've been going back and forth all night,' he says. 'Good. That means you're thinking. Most people pick a side in the first five minutes and spend the rest of their lives defending it.'

He drinks. 'Here's what I've learned in twenty years of writing about politics, religion, and human stupidity: the truth is usually uncomfortable, plural, and ugly. Holmes is right that courage is real. Wilson is right that tyranny should be resisted. Butler is right that someone's getting rich. Remarque is right that it's hell.'

'The propagandist takes one of those truths and pretends it's the only truth. The honest man holds all of them and still acts.'

'So, Theo. What will you do?'

choices.text: 'I'll hold all the truths and still think for myself.'
'I'll write — like you do — to help others see clearly.'
'I don't know. It's too much. I can't decide.'
'The loudest voice must be right. Wilson's the President.'

end_critical_thinker.title: The Clear-Eyed Citizen
end_critical_thinker.text:
Theo wakes at his library desk. The fluorescent lights are back. The essay on American neutrality lies half-written.

But everything has changed. He picks up his pen and writes — not what the textbook says, not what any single voice told him, but what he actually thinks, having heard them all.

He writes about Holmes's courage and the danger of romanticizing violence. About Wilson's ideals and the cost of crusades. About Butler's numbers and the limits of cynicism. About Remarque's mud and the truth that no speech can erase.

He writes about **rhetoric** — how every speaker uses language to shape what you believe, and how the only defense is to read carefully, think critically, and never surrender your judgment to anyone else's eloquence.

Mencken's voice echoes in his memory: 'The honest man holds all the truths and still acts.'

Theo hands in his essay. It is the best thing he has ever written.

vocabulary.definition: The art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing — a tool that can illuminate truth or obscure it

end_mencken_disciple.title: The Sage's Apprentice
end_mencken_disciple.text:
Theo wakes at his desk with Mencken's voice still ringing: 'The writer's job is to comfort the afflicted and afflict the comfortable.'

He picks up his pen — and instead of the assigned essay, he writes something dangerous. An editorial. About how politicians use beautiful language to sell ugly things. About how newspapers print propaganda and call it patriotism. About how the first casualty of war is not truth — it's the audience's willingness to think.

The essay earns him a C-minus and a note from his teacher: 'Interesting but too cynical. Try to be more balanced.'

Theo smiles. Balance is for people who haven't been paying attention. He writes another editorial. And another. And another.

Somewhere, the ghost of a round man in spectacles approves.

end_wilsonian_idealist.title: The Honest Crusader
end_wilsonian_idealist.text:
Theo wakes at his desk, and the world feels urgent. Democracy is not a word to him anymore — it's something that must be actively defended, with clear eyes and full knowledge of the cost.

He writes his essay about Wilson's war address, but not the essay his teacher expected. He writes about Wilson's rhetorical brilliance — the anaphora, the euphemisms, the appeal to civilization — and then he writes about what Wilson didn't say: the numbers who would die, the peace that would be botched, the ideals that would be betrayed.

'I believe in Wilson's cause,' Theo writes, 'but I refuse to believe in Wilson's language. The cause deserves honest words, not pretty ones.'

It is a good essay — thoughtful, passionate, and aware of its own contradictions. Mencken would not approve entirely. But he might nod, just once.

end_antiwar_crusader.title: The Dissenter
end_antiwar_crusader.text:
Theo wakes at his desk with Butler's numbers burned into his mind. Twenty-one thousand new millionaires. He writes his essay about the economics of war — who profits, who pays, who dies.

But Mencken's warning stays with him too. He doesn't reduce war to mere economics. He acknowledges that some soldiers fought for real convictions, that some causes may genuinely justify sacrifice. He just insists that the public has a right to know the full ledger — not just the glory, but the bill.

His teacher is uncomfortable. 'This is well-argued,' she says carefully, 'but it's a controversial position.'

'Good,' Theo says, surprising himself. 'Controversial is just the word people use when they don't want to think about the argument.'

He's quoting Mencken. He doesn't mind.

end_martial_romantic.title: Touched with Fire
end_martial_romantic.text:
Theo wakes at his desk with Holmes's words ringing like a bell: 'Through our great good fortune, in our youth our hearts were touched with fire.'

He writes his essay about duty, sacrifice, and the nobility of answering a call greater than yourself. It's passionate and sincere.

But somewhere in the back of his mind, Mencken's question lingers: 'Which truths are you choosing to ignore right now?'

Theo pushes the question away. The essay gets an A. His teacher loves it. His classmates are moved.

The question stays, though — quiet, persistent, unanswered. Theo chose the poetry. Whether he chose wisely, only time and experience will tell.

end_propagandist.title: The Craftsman
end_propagandist.text:
Theo wakes at his desk with a new understanding of power. Not physical power — rhetorical power. He now knows how Wilson moved a nation to war, how Holmes made death beautiful, how Butler turned patriotism into a dirty word.

And he knows he can do it too.

He writes his essay — and it's brilliant. Every technique Mencken taught him is deployed with precision. Anaphora for rhythm. Register mixing for surprise. Carefully chosen abstractions to hide what he doesn't want the reader to see.

The essay says what his teacher wants to hear, in exactly the words that will earn the best grade. It's a perfect piece of persuasion.

It's also, Theo realizes with a small chill, a perfect piece of propaganda. He has become exactly what Mencken warned him about. He just can't remember when it happened.

end_silenced.title: The Paralysis of Knowledge
end_silenced.text:
Theo wakes at his desk. The essay is still blank.

He knows too much now. Every sentence he writes, he hears Mencken dissecting it. Every argument, he sees the counter-argument. Every word, he suspects of being a rhetorical trick.

Holmes is right, but also wrong. Wilson is sincere, but also manipulative. Butler has the numbers, but misses the human element. Remarque has the truth, but it leads to despair.

Theo stares at the blank page. The clock ticks. He picks up his pen, writes a sentence, crosses it out. Writes another. Crosses it out.

Knowledge without courage is paralysis. Mencken knew that — he wrote fiercely, even knowing he might be wrong. Theo learned the tools but not the nerve to use them.

The page stays blank.

end_true_believer.title: The Willing Subject
end_true_believer.text:
Theo wakes at his desk. The essay comes easily — he parrots Wilson's words, echoes the newspaper editorials, and fills two pages with borrowed convictions.

He never heard Holmes, or Butler, or Remarque. He never sat with Mencken. He picked the loudest, most authoritative voice in the room and repeated it back.

The essay gets a B-plus. His teacher writes 'Good job!' in red ink. Theo feels satisfied.

Somewhere in 1917, a man in round spectacles shakes his head. 'The **booboisie** grows by one,' he mutters, and goes back to his typewriter.

vocabulary.definition: Mencken's term for the gullible masses who accept whatever the loudest authority tells them
